region_of_origin: Central and South America (widely cultivated globally)

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw green bell pepper weighs approximately 119 g and is commonly consumed whole as a snack, sliced into strips for dipping, or chopped for use in salads and cooked dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Bell peppers, sweet, green, raw — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11333)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for sweet green pepper (NDB 11333) for secondary nutrient completeness. HPB values used where available; USDA SR Legacy used to fill saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamin C, vitamin A, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Green bell pepper GI is not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the published GI range for non-starchy vegetables with very low carbohydrate content (2.5g/100g), consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of non-starchy vegetables as low GI (typically 10–20). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 2.975) / 100 ≈ 0.4, using 119g serving size (2.5g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.19 = 2.975g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1 mg/100g) and high potassium (190 mg/100g) providing a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Green bell pepper is an excellent choice for hypertension management due to its negligible sodium content (1 mg/100g) and meaningful potassium contribution (190 mg/100g), which supports vascular tone and blood pressure regulation; consume raw or lightly cooked without added salt or high-sodium sauces to preserve its cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.5g/100g), low glycemic index (~15), and dietary fibre (1.5g/100g) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Green bell pepper has a very low glycemic index (~15) and minimal carbohydrate load (2.5g/100g), making it an ideal vegetable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its dietary fibre slows glucose absorption and its high vitamin C content (~80 mg/100g) may support insulin sensitivity, but avoid pairing with high-sugar dips or sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.03g/100g), zero cholesterol, high vitamin C (~80 mg/100g), and potassium (190 mg/100g) supporting endothelial and cardiac health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Green bell pepper is cardioprotective due to its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high antioxidant vitamin C content (~80 mg/100g), which reduces oxidative LDL damage; its potassium content supports healthy blood pressure and its dietary fibre contributes to favourable lipid profiles when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable (Solanaceae family) containing solanine alkaloids that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Green bell pepper is a nightshade vegetable and RA-sensitive individuals should trial a 4–6 week elimination period to assess personal tolerance; if no symptom worsening is observed upon reintroduction, moderate consumption may be acceptable, and alternative vitamin C sources (citrus, kiwi) should be considered if nightshades remain problematic.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green bell pepper (Capsicum annuum) in its raw state is a crisp, bright-green non-starchy vegetable belonging to the Solanaceae (nightshade) family, widely consumed across global cuisines including Singaporean, Chinese, Malay, Indian, Western, and Mediterranean cooking. Raw green bell pepper has a firm, crunchy texture and a mildly bitter, slightly grassy flavour that distinguishes it from the sweeter red and yellow varieties, which are simply more mature versions of the same fruit. Nutritionally, raw green bell pepper is exceptionally low in calories (16 kcal/100g) and carbohydrates (2.5g/100g), with negligible fat (0g/100g) and modest protein (0.9g/100g). Its dietary fibre content (1.5g/100g) supports digestive health and glycaemic regulation. It is an outstanding source of vitamin C (~80 mg/100g, approximately 89% of the adult daily reference intake), which functions as a potent antioxidant supporting immune function, collagen synthesis, and endothelial health. Its potassium content (190 mg/100g) and near-zero sodium (1 mg/100g) make it particularly beneficial for blood pressure management. The glycemic index of green bell pepper is very low (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Clinically, it is appropriate for hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes dietary plans. However, as a nightshade vegetable, it may be contraindicated for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to solanine alkaloids. Green bell pepper is consumed raw in salads, crudités, and as a snack with dips, and is also widely used cooked in stir-fries, stuffed pepper dishes, curries, and mixed vegetable preparations across Asian and Western cuisines.
common_user_queries: Is green bell pepper good for diabetes?
How many calories in a green bell pepper?
Is green pepper good for blood pressure?
Can I eat green bell pepper if I have rheumatoid arthritis?
Green capsicum vs red capsicum nutrition
Is raw green pepper high in vitamin C?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11333) for sweet green pepper. Both sources are approved primary sources. Values are consistent between sources. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database classification of non-starchy vegetables, clearly documented as an estimate.
notes: Fat_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is consistent with USDA SR Legacy which reports total fat of 0.17g/100g for raw green sweet pepper — the HPB value rounds to 0.0 at one decimal place. Fat_g is effectively naturally zero / trace for this food. Saturated fat (0.03g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.0g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.06g), and trans_fat_g (0.0g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11333) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. Vitamin C (80.4 mg), vitamin A (18 mcg RAE), B-vitamins, vitamin D (0.0 mcg), vitamin E (0.37 mg), phosphorus (20 mg), magnesium (10 mg), zinc (0.13 mg), selenium (0.1 mcg), and water (93.9g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11333). Cholesterol is confirmed zero per both HPB and USDA SR Legacy — naturally zero as a plant food. Vitamin B12 (0.0 mcg) and vitamin D (0.0 mcg) are naturally zero as this is a raw plant food with no animal-derived components. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for green bell pepper was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches based on published ontology documentation; FoodEx2 code A00GJ is used as the closest defensible match for sweet pepper, green, raw in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Rheumatoid arthritis health context included as an additional condition given the nightshade classification of green bell pepper and its clinical relevance for RA patients.
